result:
:::writing
The night bus hums like it knows a secret and refuses to share it.

Fluorescent lights buzz overhead, turning everyone into a slightly different version of themselves—paler, quieter, suspended between stops. A man in the back cradles a paper bag as if it might breathe if he holds it gently enough. Two seats ahead, a woman watches her reflection in the dark window instead of the city sliding past it, as though she’s waiting for it to blink first.

Every stop is a small act of faith. The doors open. Someone leaves. Someone else gets on. The driver never turns around.

Outside, the city rearranges itself into brief, flickering stories: a laundromat glowing like an aquarium; a couple arguing in the blue light of a vending machine; a stray cat threading its way between parked cars with the confidence of something that belongs everywhere and nowhere.

Inside, time stretches thin. You could believe this bus has always been moving, that it will always be moving, that the route is a circle so large it looks like a straight line.

At some point—no one notices exactly when—the man with the paper bag relaxes his grip. The woman at the window looks away. Someone pulls the cord, and the bell dings like a small, polite revelation.

The bus exhales. The doors open. The night takes another piece of itself back.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
